posts.name: Shlomo Silverstein
posts.cooked: I have the opportunity to pickup a 2021 Puligny-Montrachet Village wine from Domaine Leflaive or a 2020 1er Les Pucelles from Drouhin for the same price. Which would you all recommend. I have tried neither before but like both producers in general.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Not very excited about either TBH. Probably the Drouhin simply because it’s a 2020.

posts.name: Ryan A.ns.el
posts.cooked:
I had the ‘21s in barrel and D. Leflaive was one of the standouts in ‘21 across their portfolio but I did not try the PM village… so not much help specific to that wine.

Agree the 2020 vintage is superior but not all things created equal.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: I’d go Leflaive for sure, even though I like Drouhin as well. Leflaive has been firing on all cylinders and pricing has only been going up.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Hence why PM is the same price as Pucelles?

posts.name: Shlomo Silverstein
posts.cooked: Leflaive is one of my favorites … The prices are starting to become outrageous. I was surprised to see it costing as much as pucelles. Was curious if anyone has had these two before (maybe years different) . Never had leflaive village and drouhin pucelles but have had others from those producers and have loved both.

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked: If you plan on aging it there’s only one answer - Leflaive is under Diam and Drouhin is not.

posts.name: Shlomo Silverstein
posts.cooked: Good point. Will probably let it sit for at least 7-8 yrs

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Buy one of each. Otherwise, you will never learn how to make these determinations for yourself in the future.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Ardeis Scott
posts.cooked: Is the only way to know if a wine is "corked with DIAM is pull the foil and look or open one? Wineries don’t provide that info do they?

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
Ardeis_Scott:

Is the only way to know if a wine is "corked with DIAM is pull the foil and look or open one? Wineries don’t provide that info do they?

Theoretically … you should be able to ask a retailer to ask the distributor.

If you’re looking at buying online … I’m not sure how up to date this wiki is, but this is also a good start: start [Oxidized Burgundies]

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked: It depends on what style you prefer. I can’t stand Leflaive and quite like Drouhin, so it’s an easy choice for me, regardless of closure. They’re really quite different wines, so if you like the heavy Leflaive style then that’s what I’d go for.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Don’t think “heavy” characterizes the style today…

The Drouhin house style is certainly fatter/richer/broader in white today than Leflaive in the Pierre Vincent era.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
Perhaps heavy is the wrong word to use - indeed they’re not heavy in the way they used to be (though I do really like older, correct bottles). That said, I can’t get around having this association with the wines. Perhaps it’s the forceful sense of power that they often express. There haven’t been many recent bottles of Leflaive that I’ve liked (including from verticals of 17 and 20); apart perhaps from some of the Macconais bottlings which have been enjoyable enough. We drank the 17 BBM again a few weeks ago and I actually gave my glass away. The person I gave it to loved the wine so I thought it was better off with him. I guess I just don’t like this particular brand of power in a wine. Also, I feel like the oak is often detectable in a rather unpleasant way, even though there’s not very much of it any more.

While the Drouhin may certainly be a richer wine in many ways, I expect I would enjoy it more. I’m going to have to buy these now to try to explain why
